title: Self-consistent analysis of the Kuramoto model with higher-order interactions
abstract: The Kuramoto model with higher-order interactions has recently been shown to exhibit bistability, explosive synchronization transitions, and rich collective dynamics. Existing analytical approaches, however, typically rely on all-to-all coupling or mean-field approximations of the underlying hypergraph structure. While these methods describe typical networks in the thermodynamic limit, they generally fail to capture the effects of finite hypergraph and oscillator frequency realizations. To address this limitation, we develop a self-consistent analytical framework for the Kuramoto model with dyadic and triadic interactions on hypergraphs. We introduce generalized local order parameters that capture the combined effects of dyadic and triadic phase correlations, and derive a hierarchy of approximation schemes for the local and global synchronization order parameters. Using these approximations, we determine critical coupling strengths for the onset of synchronization and bistability. In particular, we show that the critical triadic coupling strength governing the onset of bistability depends on correlations between the eigenvectors of the dyadic adjacency matrix and the triadic interaction structure. Numerical simulations on homogeneous and heterogeneous hypergraphs validate the theory and illustrate the distinct regimes of applicability of the approximation schemes.
summary: This paper provides a new analytical framework to study synchronization in systems with multi-body interactions. It reveals that the stability of the synchronized state can be controlled by tuning the structural correlations between pair-wise and triplet-wise connections in a hypergraph.
main_problem: Developing a self-consistent analytical framework to understand synchronization transitions, bistability, and explosive synchronization in the Kuramoto model with higher-order (triadic) interactions on hypergraphs.
main_result: The authors derive a hierarchy of approximations (TAT, FDA, HMF) and show that the onset of bistability depends on the correlation between the dyadic adjacency matrix eigenvectors and the triadic interaction structure.
method: A self-consistent analytical framework using a hierarchy of approximations (Time-Averaged Theory, Frequency Distribution Approximation, and Heterogeneous Mean-Field) combined with perturbative expansion and numerical simulations.
model_or_system: A generalized Kuramoto model of coupled phase oscillators on hypergraphs, featuring both dyadic (pair-wise) and triadic (three-body) interactions with intrinsic frequencies drawn from a distribution.
key_observables: Global and local order parameters (dyadic and triadic), critical coupling strengths (K2 and K3), and synchronization response (S).
important_parameters: Dyadic and triadic coupling strengths (K2, K3), intrinsic frequencies (omega), hypergraph degree distributions, and eigenvector correlations.
main_assumptions: Assumes a single synchronization cluster, neglects pair correlations, assumes statistical independence between frequencies and local order parameters, and assumes a symmetric frequency distribution.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate how triplet arrangement and degree correlations affect the bistability threshold, compare the accuracy of different approximation schemes (TAT, FDA, HMF) against simulations, and demonstrate structural control of synchronization via hypergraph rewiring.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the higher-order Kuramoto model, develops a hierarchy of three analytical approximations (TAT, FDA, HMF), uses perturbative expansion to find critical thresholds, validates the theory via numerical simulations on various hypergraph topologies, and demonstrates how network rewiring can control synchronization regimes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Effect of hypergraph structure on Kc 3. (Top panels) Nodes {n, m, j} have high values of Vn, Um, Uj, respectively (e.g., by having large dyadic degrees), while nodes {n′, m′, j′} have low values of Vn′, Um′, Uj′. Configurations where hyperedges couple oscillator triplets {n, m, j} and {n′, m′, j′} (a) have lower values of Kc 3 than those where those hyperedges couple other triplets, {n, m, j′} and {n′, m′, j} (b). (Bottom panels) Visualizations of hypergraphs where triadic and dyadic degrees are either correlated (c) or anti-correlated (d).

Figure 2: Global order parameter R(2) as a function of K2/Kc 2 for uniform (top) and power-law (bottom) degree distributions (N = 20, 000, ⟨kn⟩= 500). Panels (a) and (c) show the weak triadic coupling regime (K3 < Kc 3), while (b) and (d) show the strong triadic coupling regime (K3 > Kc 3). Solid and dashed lines denote forward and backward parameter sweeps, respectively.
Figure 3: Examples of small network experiments with N = 1000, ⟨k⟩= ⟨q⟩= 25, γ = 2.5 with kMAX = 125.

Figure 4: Synchronization response S calculated for a power-law degree distribution with γ = 3.0 and N = 3000. The dyadic coupling is fixed at K2 = 0.95Kc 2. (a) shows the system below the onset of bistability with K3 = 0.5Kc 3, where the order parameter remains near zero. (b) shows the system in the bistable regime with K3 = 1.25Kc 3.

Figure 5: Synchronization response S (indicating bistability) for different degree distributions as a function of K3/Kc 3. Panels (a)-(c) show power-law distributions, while (d) shows the uniform case.
